coredata.dc:title: Electrical stimulation enables dynamic regulation of the tribological behaviors of polyelectrolyte-modified carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Mou, Zihao
Peng, Jie
Yan, Ruixin
Yang, Qingbin
Zhao, Bin
Xiao, Dan
coredata.dc:description: Dynamic and active control of the tribological behaviors of lubricant additives under electrical stimulation is important for the development of electromechanical devices. Carbon dots (CDs) show great prospects in electrical contact lubrication owing to their easily acquired oil dispersibility, ionic conductivity, and high lubricity. Herein, the polyelectrolyte-modified CDs (PCDs) were synthesized from the chitosan-derived polyamine-terminated CDs. The PCDs can be stabilized in polyethylene glycol (PEG200) synthetic oil for more than one year without any precipitates and fluorescence decay. Compared with neat PEG200, the formulated PCDs (0.25 wt%)/PEG200 lubricant achieved 63.6% and 39.1% reductions in wear and friction, respectively. In addition, the durability of PEG200 also can be enhanced by PCDs. Crucially, the friction regulation of PCDs under external electric fields (as low as ±1.0 V) was easily realized for the first time, which effectively helps to reveal the indispensable role of polyelectrolyte shells in improving the tribological performances of PCDs. Experiments and characterizations demonstrated that the superior tribological peculiarities of PCDs benefited from the collaborations of their polyelectrolyte shells and carbon cores. The findings provide a universal protocol and material support for expediting the potential-controlled lubrication progress of nano-additives.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrical stimulation
Polyelectrolytes
Carbon dots
Lubricant additives
Tribological properties

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Tribological properties of PCDs as the lubricant additives of PEG200

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthetic routes of CDs and PCDs. TEM and HRTEM images of (b) CDs and (c) PCDs. (d) XRD patterns of CDs and PCDs. The insets in panels a and b display the HRTEM images of single CDs and PCDs, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) FTIR and (b) full-scan XPS spectra of CDs and PCDs. (c) C1s, (d) O1s, (e) N1s, and (f) B1s XPS fine spectra of PCDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Optical photographs of the treated PCDs (0.10 wt%)/PEG200 lubricants under (a) sunlight and (b) 365 nm UV light, respectively. (c) COF curves, (d) mean wear volumes and COFs of PEG200 with incremental c of PCDs. Cross-sectional, 2D and 3D wear profiles of the rubbed surfaces lubricated by (e) PEG200 and (f) PCDs (0.25 wt%)/PEG200 lubricant, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Mean COFs and wear volumes of PEG200 and PCDs (0.25 wt%)/PEG200 lubricant under various (a, c) vertical loads and (b, d) sliding speeds, respectively. (e) COF curves, (f) mean COFs and wear volumes of PEG200 and PCDs (0.25 wt%)/PEG200 lubricant under the prolonged friction duration, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Ceramic/steel contact friction configuration with electrical stimulation. COF curves of (b, c) PEG200 and (d, e) PCDs (0.25 wt%)/PEG200 lubricant under alternately applying ±1.0 and ± 3.0 V, respectively. (f) The proposed lubrication model of PCDs under electrical stimulation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
SEM images of the rubbed surfaces on the steel plate lubricated with (a) PEG200 and (b) PCDs (0.25 wt%)/PEG200 lubricant, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

XPS (a) full-scan and high-resolution (b) Fe2p, (c) C1s, (d) O1s, (e) N1s, and (f) B1s spectra of the rubbed surfaces on the steel plate lubricated with PEG200 and PCDs (0.25 wt%)/PEG200 lubricant, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images of the FIB-lifted slice from the rubbed surface on the steel plate lubricated with PCDs (0.25 wt%)/PEG200 lubricant; (c) FFT analysis, (d) HADDF image, and affiliated EDS elemental mappings for the selected area from the segment, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Lubrication mechanism of PCDs as the lubricant additives of PEG200. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Electrical stimulation enables dynamic regulation of the tribological behaviors of polyelectrolyte-modified carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, Sichuan University, 24 Section of the Southbound 1, First Ring Road, Chengdu, 610065, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Institute for Advanced Study, Chengdu University, 2025 Chengluo Avenue, Chengdu, 610106, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dynamic and active control of the tribological behaviors of lubricant additives under electrical stimulation is important for the development of electromechanical devices. Carbon dots (CDs) show great prospects in electrical contact lubrication owing to their easily acquired oil dispersibility, ionic conductivity, and high lubricity. Herein, the polyelectrolyte-modified CDs (PCDs) were synthesized from the chitosan-derived polyamine-terminated CDs. The PCDs can be stabilized in polyethylene glycol (PEG200) synthetic oil for more than one year without any precipitates and fluorescence decay. Compared with neat PEG200, the formulated PCDs (0.25 wt%)/PEG200 lubricant achieved 63.6% and 39.1% reductions in wear and friction, respectively. In addition, the durability of PEG200 also can be enhanced by PCDs. Crucially, the friction regulation of PCDs under external electric fields (as low as ±1.0 V) was easily realized for the first time, which effectively helps to reveal the indispensable role of polyelectrolyte shells in improving the tribological performances of PCDs. Experiments and characterizations demonstrated that the superior tribological peculiarities of PCDs benefited from the collaborations of their polyelectrolyte shells and carbon cores. The findings provide a universal protocol and material support for expediting the potential-controlled lubrication progress of nano-additives.

ce:section.ce:para: Dynamic and active friction control of lubricant additives has long been a common purpose in tribology [1,2]. The external electric field is a simple and efficient tool to modulate the motion and reaction of additives by changing the friction surface potential so that the tribological behaviors of polar additives can be manipulated by the potential-controlled surface [3–6]. Over the past decades, the friction control of molecular additives has been realized by tuning the electrical potential between metallic tribo-pairs [7–11]. It is recognized that dispersibility and electrical responsiveness dominate the potential-controlled lubricity of additives [12]. So far, applying an external electric field to regulate the tribological peculiarities of nano-additives is rarely reported due to the inferior dispersion stability and electrical responsiveness of traditional nanomaterials in oils which remains the greatest challenge for achieving the efficiently potential-controlled lubrication of nano-additives [13–17].

Carbon dots (CDs), defined as a class of core-shell structured hybrids/composites containing inorganic carbon core and organic passivation shell with designable functional groups, have aroused research attention in many applications [18–20]. Owing to remarkable structural designability, tunable dispersibility, and admirable lubricity, CDs have recently been regarded as a promising alternative to conventional inorganic nano-additives [21–39]. In view of the merits of ionic liquids (ILs) in tribology, Wang and coauthors synthesized a series of CDs-based additives by integrating multiple anions-contained ILs onto the carbon core. It is demonstrated that the CDs-ILs possess superior interfacial adsorption and insertion ability on the rubbing surface, thus improving the tribological properties of polyethylene glycol (PEG) synthetic oils [21,22]. Cai and colleagues prepared several ILs-derived CDs (CDs-ILs) by pyrolyzing ILs with various anionic species. These CDs-ILs, as the additives of PEG, displayed good oil solubility and tribological properties [34]. Admittedly, the above CDs-ILs can be endowed with outstanding dispersion stability in oils, which meets the premise of potential-controlled lubrication. However, the friction control of CDs-ILs under external electrical stimulation may be restricted by the mediocre electrical responsiveness of the sparse ILs onto CDs. As expected, the simultaneous improvement of surface charge density and oil dispersibility of CDs will possibly provide a rare opportunity for expediting the potential-controlled lubrication progress of nano-additives.

Polyelectrolytes constructed by a charged polymeric skeleton and copious ionizable groups are instrumental in designing functional nanomaterials with superior ionic conductivity and dispersibility in lubricants [40–43]. Our previous studies have demonstrated that the polyelectrolytes-modified CDs (PCDs) containing oil-soluble counterions could obtain superior dispersion stability in PEG200. The PCDs as additives showed strong interfacial adsorption and film-forming function on the rubbed steel surface, thus leading to superb tribological behaviors [29,30]. Considering the admirable mechanical, thermal, chemical, and electrical properties of polyelectrolytes, the PCDs are promising to achieve high-efficiency potential-controlled lubrication. However, the practicability of the aforesaid PCDs is profoundly confined by their uneconomic and intricate preparation, modification, and purification steps. The low-cost precursors and facile synthetic processes are urgently required to be explored for synthesizing the PCDs toward potential-controlled lubrication.

Chitosan is a naturally abundant polysaccharide that can be extracted from crustaceans, bacteria, and algae. Chitosan is hydrophilic, non-toxic, degradable, and rich in nanoscience and biomedicine [44,45]. Using universal carbonization strategies, chitosan is easily dehydrated and carbonized to generate highly fluorescent CDs with polymeric shell and carbon core, widening the applications of chitosan in sensors [46], energy storage [47], anti-counterfeiting [48], electrochemical imaging [49], and so forth. Chitosan-derived CDs are often called polyamine-functionalized CDs or polycationic CDs owing to the existence of copious amino in the polymeric shell, which provides a myriad of active sites to synthesize PCDs. We previously reported that the polyethyleneimine-grafted CDs could be transformed into PCDs by simple and rapid protonation and anion exchange reactions [29,30]. Thus, the speculation that the chitosan-derived CDs can be easily modified as PCDs is adequately supported by theoretical and experimental evidence. Nonetheless, as far as we know, studies on the introduction of chitosan-based PCDs as additives for potential-controlled lubrication have not been reported.

Inspired by the above analyses, here we successfully synthesized a kind of halogen-free PCDs from the chitosan-derived CDs. The physicochemical properties of PCDs were fully revealed by a series of microstructural, spectral, and thermal analyses. The dispersibility and long-term stability of PCDs in PEG200 base oil were estimated. The impacts of testing parameters on the anti-wear and friction-reducing performances of PCDs were systematically evaluated in the steel/steel contact tribo-pairs constituted by reciprocally sliding ball-on-plate configuration. The potential-controlled lubricity of PCDs was further explored in the ceramic/steel contact tribo-pairs under electrical stimulation. The lubrication mechanism of PCDs was elucidated by summarizing experiments and characterizations.

Concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36.0–38.0%), absolute ethanol (≥99.7%, AR grade), and hydrogen peroxide solution (H2O2, 31.0% in water) were purchased from Chengdu Jinshan Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd (China). Chitosan (RG grade, degree of deacetylation ≥90.0%) and polyethylene glycol (PEG200, AR grade) with an average molecular weight of 200.0 g/mol were supplied by Shanghai Adamas-Beta Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd (China). All chemicals and reagents were used as received. The bis(salicylate)borate lithium (Li–BScB) was prepared by blending salicylic acid (2.76 g, 20 mmol) in the aqueous solution of boric acid (0.62 g, 10 mmol) and lithium carbonate (0.37 g, 5 mmol) at 60 °C for 3 h [50].

The amino-terminated CDs were firstly synthesized by a green solvothermal route we reported recently, as depicted in Fig. 1 a [37]. Typically, chitosan (0.5 g) and ethanol (30 ml) were mixed and sealed into a Teflon-lined reactor containing 3 ml of H2O2 as the clean oxidant. The reactor was maintained at 200 °C for 12 h. After cooling, the resulting reddish-brown suspension was filtered and centrifuged at 12,000 rpm to isolate the solid residues. A fuscous and viscous crude product was obtained by separating the solvent in the filtrate. The crude product was redispersed and dialyzed against regularly changed ultrapure water for 4 days to eliminate the molecular impurities. The CDs with a yield of 17.8% were collected as brown powder by freeze-drying.

The CDs were transformed into hydrophobic PCDs by the protonation and anion exchange reaction in water. Above all, 0.5 g of CDs were protonated with 25 ml of 0.1 M HCl aqueous solution under stirring overnight. The cloudy black-brown suspension was formed and centrifuged to collect the filtrate containing the protonated CDs. Then, 1.0 g of Li–BScB was poured into the filtrate under stirring for 2 h. The PCDs were obtained as precipitates and lyophilized for further use.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were captured by a JEOL JEM 2100F system. X-ray diffraction (XRD) profiles were collected from a Bruker D8 Advance X’ Pert Pro diffractometer from 5 to 80° with Cu Kα radiation. Fourier transforms infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained from a WQF-520 spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted by an ESCALAB 250XI photoelectron spectrometer with a monochromatized Al Kα X-ray radioactive source (1486.71 eV). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed on a STA449F3 detector under a shielding N2 atmosphere with a heating rate of 10 °C/min.

The tribological performances of lubricants were tested by using the HSR-2M reciprocally high-speed friction and wear tester equipped with a ball-on-plate module. The steel/steel contact configuration was constructed by vertically loading the stationary steel ball (GCr15, hardness of about 60 HRC, diameter of 6 mm) against the sliding steel plate (GCr15, hardness of about 60 HRC, average roughness of 17.1 nm (Fig. S1), dimensions of Φ8 × 8 × 2 mm) below. Each experiment was performed three times under the default parameters (sliding speeds, 2–10 cm/s; vertical loads, 15–75 N; stroke, 5 mm; duration, 30 and 250 min; ambient temperature and humidity) to collect credible and repeatable results. Before testing, 0.3 ml of sample was carefully injected into the ball/plate contact zone. The real-time COF curves and values were automatically collected by the tester equipped with a sensitive data acquisition system. The wear profiles and volumes of wear tracks were obtained by scanning and reconstructing the entire rubbed surfaces using a 3D profilometer (CHOTEST, Super View W1) based on white-light interferometry.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were recorded by a JEOL JSM-6710F system to detect the microstructural and morphological features of the rubbed surfaces. The elemental species and distributions on the rubbed surfaces were probed by energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS). The elemental compositions and valence states of the tribo-products on the rubbed surfaces were confirmed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) on an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) linked with EDS was executed on an FEI Tecnai F20 system to reveal the nanostructured information of the protective tribo-film formed on the rubbed surface. The sample for HRTEM analyses was achieved by a focused ion beam (FIB) on a Helios 600i instrument to separate a segment from the cross-section of the rubbed surface.

The TEM image displayed in Fig. 1b confirms the formation of uniform and quasi-spherical CDs with a mean diameter of 4.9 nm. The mean diameters of CDs and PCDs were estimated by the statistical distribution of hundred particles. Unexpectedly, the morphology, distribution, dimension (5.0 nm), and lattice fringe (0.24 nm) of PCDs shown in Fig. 1c are almost the same as CDs, disclosing that the morphological, structural, and crystalline features of CDs are not transformed by the surface modification. Crucially, the core-shell structure of CDs and PCDs can be distinctly discerned from the HRTEM images. The crystalline properties of CDs and PCDs were determined by powder XRD patterns. As exhibited in Fig. 1d, a broad diffraction peak at a 2θ of 21.5° matching with an interlayer spacing of 0.41 nm can be assigned to the (002) lattice plane of graphitic carbon, reflecting the good crystallinity of CDs [51]. The peak of PCDs slightly shifts to a 2θ of 21.3°, proving the little impact of surface anions on the crystallinity of CDs.

The chemical compositions of CDs and PCDs were analyzed by FTIR spectra. As displayed in Fig. 2 a, the bands at about 3421 and 1644 cm−1 can be ascribed to the typical stretching vibration of N–H (–NH2) and N–H (OC–NH), respectively, which proves the retainment of amino and the formation of amide onto CDs [52]. Two distinct signals at 2926 and 1376 cm−1 represent the C–H (–CH3/–CH2–) stretching and C–H (–CH–OH) deformation vibrations, reflecting the survival of polymeric skeleton onto CDs [53]. The peaks at 3445, 1669, 1470, 1326, 763, and 1136 cm−1 stand for the N–H (–NH3 +), CC, C–O, CO, B–O stretching vibrations, and C–O deformation vibration, respectively, confirming the protonation of amino and the successful introduction of the chelated orthoborate structure onto PCDs [54]. XPS measurements were performed to identify the elemental information of CDs and PCDs. As depicted in Fig. 2b, the full-scan XPS spectrum of CDs exhibits three intense peaks corresponding to C1s, O1s, and N1s. However, in addition to C1s, O1s, and N1s, the distinct binding energy of B1s can be observed from the spectrum of PCDs, indicating the PCDs are made up of B, C, N, and O elements. Compared with the fine spectra of CDs given in Fig. S2, the binding energies at 288.3 eV, 291.2 eV, 192.5 eV, and 533.8 eV shown in Fig. 2c–f can be respectively assigned to the CC–OB, O–C–OB, and O–B species, notarizing the generation of PCDs [30,54]. The above conclusions also can be drawn by the elemental contents of CDs and PCDs summarized in Table S1.

The thermal durability of CDs and PCDs was evaluated by TGA. An obvious mass loss in the range of 35–100 °C representing the vaporization of moisture can be observed from CDs, affirming the existence of copious hydrophilic hydroxy and amino onto CDs, as displayed in Fig. S3. In sharp contrast, the hydrophobic PCDs display low moisture content. The second mass loss of CDs appears at about 168 °C, which can be allocated to the decomposition of molecular fragments onto CDs. However, the starting dissociation of organic groups onto PCDs is adjacent to 205 °C, reflecting the satisfactory thermostability of polyelectrolyte shells. The terminal mass retention of PCDs is about 4.09% less than CDs, directly accounting for the immobilization of the BScB− onto PCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tribological properties of PCDs as the lubricant additives of PEG200
ce:section.ce:para: Dispersion stability is a crucial peculiarity affecting the tribological behaviors of lubricant additives [55]. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the PCDs can be easily dispersed into PEG200 to form the pellucid lubricant without macroscopic sediment after ultrasonic treatment. No obvious precipitates and color changes can be found in the lubricant after storing it at room temperature for over one year, refrigerating it at −15 °C for a month, heating it to 100 °C for two weeks, and centrifuging it at 10,000 rpm for 30 min, indicating the super dispersion stability of PCDs. As recognized, CDs are fluorescence nanomaterials with aggregation-induced quenching properties [56,57]. Amazingly, bright yellow fluorescence can be observed in all the treated lubricants under UV light of 365 nm (Fig. 3b), hinting at the stable monodisperse state of PCDs in PEG200.

The influence of the additive amount of PCDs on its tribological properties was evaluated first. Fig. 3c exhibits the evolution trends of COF curves of the lubricants with increasing concentration (c) of PCDs under a vertical load of 15 N. Distinctly, the COF curves of the PCDs-based lubricants with incremental c are lower than that of PEG200, demonstrating the superior friction-reducing function of PCDs. The lubricant containing 0.25 wt% of PCDs provides the lowest COF curve without a visible running-in stage, confirming the optimal content of PCDs. As illustrated by the reductions of 63.6% and 39.1% in the mean wear volume and COF of PEG200, respectively, the optimal c of PCDs can be further identified in Fig. 3d. Dramatically, the c of PCDs over the optimum can negatively affect its tribological behaviors, which can be explained by the abrasive wear caused by the agglomeration of excess PCDs [58,59]. The above results also can be consolidated by the cross-sectional, 2D, and 3D wear profiles given in Fig. 3e and f. Evidently, the wear track lubricated with PEG200 is deeper, wider, and rougher than that achieved by the lubricant with suitable c, intuitively reflecting the admirable anti-wear ability of PCDs.

To explore the potential of PCDs as lubricant additives, the tribological performances of PCDs (0.25 wt%)/PEG200 lubricant was investigated in wide ranges of sliding speeds and vertical loads, respectively. As displayed in Fig. S4, the COF curves of PEG200 erratically fluctuate under various conditions, demonstrating the inferior lubricity and film-forming ability of PEG200. Compared with pure PEG200, the COF curves of lubricant are relatively low and more stable, proving the remarkable load-bearing capacity and shearing strength of PCDs as nanoscale “ball-bearings”. As revealed in Fig. 4 a–d, the mean COFs of PEG200 under various parameters hover around 0.16. For comparison, the mean COFs of lubricant increase slowly from 0.097 to 0.118 and 0.115 with enlarging speeds and loads, respectively, resulting in the friction reductions attenuating to 28.2% at 10 cm/s and 27.8% at 75 N, which is closely associated with the failure of tribo-film at the greater shear force and Hertz contact stress. As expected, the wear volumes achieved by PEG200 and lubricant increase gradually with growing speeds and loads, illustrating the common features of lubricants under harsh working circumstances. As a result, the wear reductions of the lubricant are maintained in the range of 23.1–46.4% under higher loads (30, 45, 60, and 75 N) and 21.3–42.0% under faster speeds (4, 6, 8, and 10 cm/s), respectively, reflecting the satisfactory anti-wear function of PCDs. Additionally, all the mean wear volumes, COFs, and affiliated error bars of PEG200 are bigger than those of the lubricant under the whole parameters, disclosing the outstanding tribological behaviors of PCDs. The above results illustrate that the PCDs is a superior lubricant additive.

Durability is an essential property of additives for practical tribological application [60,61]. The service life of PCDs was estimated by only extending the friction duration from 30 min to 250 min. As exhibited in Fig. 4e, the COF curve of PEG200 is always at a higher level and fluctuates greatly. In contrast, the COF curve of the lubricant displays small undulations, elucidating the superior film-forming ability of PCDs. Moreover, the average wear volumes and COFs of PEG200 are reduced by 59.1% and 29.8%, respectively (Fig. 4f), proving that the lubricity of PCDs can be well-maintained in long-term use. The above conclusion also can be intuitively reflected by the cross-sectional, 2D, and 3D profiles of wear tracks shown in Fig. S5. Undoubtedly, the eminent tribological properties of PCDs as additives are closely related to the formation of the PCDs-embedded tribo-film and its nano-lubrication effects.

To disclose the potential impacts of polyelectrolyte shells on the tribological performance of PCDs, we attempted to explore the regularity of friction evolution of the PCDs-based lubricant under external electrical stimulation. As shown in Fig. 5 a, a ceramic/steel contact tribo-pair is constructed by replacing the steel ball with the same-sized Si3N4 ball to prevent short circuits. The steel holder of the Si3N4 ball and steel plate is linked with an external direct current (DC) power to constitute a two-electrode system, in which the lubricant acts as an electrolyte solution. As shown in Fig. 5e, the COF curves of the lubricant suddenly jumped up to 0.14 from 0.09 when −3.0 V was applied to the steel plate. However, the COF curve could immediately return to the initial level when the voltage was unloaded. After switching +3.0 V to the steel plate, the COF curve displayed a continuous reduction-recovery process. Similar results can be obtained at the lower voltage of ±1.0 V, as depicted in Fig. 5d. In comparison, no distinct electrical responsiveness can be observed when the uncharged PEG200 was employed as a lubricative medium (Fig. 5b and c).

The above phenomenon can be well explained by the proposed model shown in Fig. 5f. It is generally believed that the tribological properties of conductive lubricants could be regulated by adjusting the surface potential of metallic tribo-pairs. As expected, the PCDs have a brighter prospect in electric contact lubrication owing to the superior conductivity and electrical responsiveness of their polyelectrolyte shells with ionic structures. Under steel/steel contact lubrication accompanied by the escape of low-energy electrons, the PCDs as “ball-bearings” exhibited strong interfacial adsorption on the friction surfaces with positive charge density, providing favorable lubricity and load-bearing function. Under steel/ceramic contact lubrication, the rubbed steel plate applied by a positive potential can generate a higher positive charge density, inducing the aggregation of PCDs for a short-term improvement of tribological properties of lubricant and then causing the generation of abrasive wear in the friction region. Upon applying a negative potential, the PCDs tend to be repulsed by the friction surfaces with a higher negative charge density, which dramatically induces the lubrication failure of PCDs. Obviously, the admirable dispersibility, electrical responsiveness, and lubricity make the PCDs to be a promising lubricant additive with potential-controllable tribological behaviors.

SEM images were employed to visualize the wear condition and elemental information on the rubbed surfaces. As shown in Fig. 6 a, severe scratches, deep grooves, and plastic deformation can be observed from the wider and rougher surface lubricated with PEG200, intuitively reflecting the inferior lubricity of PEG200. In addition, obvious abrasive dusts can be discerned from the periphery of the rubbed surfaces lubricated by PEG200, suggesting the occurrence of abrasive wear. In sharp contrast, a few scratches can be seen in the narrower and smoother surface lubricated with the PCDs-based lubricant (Fig. 6b), directly accounting for the admirable polishing and mending effects of PCDs. EDS mappings shown in Fig. S6 and elemental contents summarized in Table S2 confirm uniform distributions and high fractions of B, C, N, and O elements on the rubbed surface lubricated with the lubricant, demonstrating the formation of PCDs-deposited tribo-film.

XPS analysis was performed to disclose the elemental species and chemical components on the rubbed surfaces. As shown in Fig. 7 a and Table S3, the peaks of C1s, O1s, and Fe2p indicate that the major elements on the rubbed surface lubricated with PEG200 are C, O, and Fe. Apparent N1s and B1s signals can be detected in the rubbed surface lubricated by the PCD-based lubricant, validating the construction of the PCDs-deposited transfer film. The subpeaks of Fe2+ (710.6, 718.8, and 727.5 eV), Fe3+ (713.8, 724.3, and 732.3 eV), and Fe–O (529.5 eV) corresponded to iron oxides can be seen from the Fe2p (Fig. 7b) and O1s (Fig. 7d) spectra, confirming the chemical oxidation of steel surface [62]. The oxidative degradation of organic moieties and the formation of friction compounds also can be determined by the CO (287.9 eV), C–O/C–N (285.5 eV), C–C/CC (284.2 eV), and Fe–C (282.7 eV) bonds in the C1s (Fig. 7c) spectra and the CO (532.2 eV) and C–O (531.0 eV) species in the O1s spectra [63]. For N1s (Fig. 7e) and B1s (Fig. 7f) spectra, no distinct signals were observed on the rubbed surface lubricated with PEG200, while the binding energies of pyrrolic N (398.8 eV), graphitic N (399.7 eV), B–O (191.8 eV) of anions, and B2O3 (190.4 eV) can be seen from the rubbed surface lubricated with the lubricant, further affirming the formation of tribo-film caused by the coupling of tribo-physical deposition and tribo-chemical reaction during friction process.

FIB-TEM was used to analyze the microstructural features of the tribo-film formed on the rubbed surface lubricated with the PCDs-based lubricant. As depicted in Fig. 8 a, a heterogeneous tribo-film with a mean thickness of about 70 nm can be observed between the Pt capping layer and the rubbed surface. Many crystals (diameter of about 5.0 nm and interlayer spacing of about 0.241 nm) and amorphous components can be detected from the HRTEM image (Fig. 8b) of the tribo-film matrix, confirming the deposition of PCDs and PEG200 on the rubbed surface. A few particles (lattice fringe of about 0.208 nm) and aggregates representing iron oxides, abrasive dust, and tribo-products also can be found and further identified by the Fourier infrared transform (FFT) analysis (Fig. 8c), intuitively reflecting the occurrence of tribo-chemical reactions on the rubbed surface [13,14]. EDS mappings (Fig. 8d) indicate the uniform distributions of B, C, N, O, and Fe elements in the tribo-film matrix, accounting for the tribo-film is virtually the PCDs-inserted tribo-chemical film, which played a crucial role in reducing wear and friction.

As revealed by correlating the tribological evaluations with the rubbed surface characterizations, the underlying lubrication mechanism of PCDs as the additives of PEG200 was put forward in Fig. 9 . At the beginning of boundary lubrication, the low-energy electrons are prone to escape from the steel/steel contact micro-convex, causing the rubbed surface to be positively charged [64]. The copious anions onto PCDs can be captured by the positively charged interface to form a molecular film by the electrostatic interaction. According to the “electroneutrality principle”, the polycationic shell-grafted carbon cores can be consecutively dragged and assembled on the rubbing interfaces to serve as scrollable “ball bearings” for polishing and mending. The above viewpoints can be well-explained by the potential-controllable friction of the PCDs-based lubricant under external electrical stimulation. As friction evolves, the organic moieties of PCDs tend to decompose and react with the exposed Fe2+/Fe3+, accounting for the generation of tribo-products. With the co-deposition of tribo-products and PCDs, a protective tribo-film embedded with PCDs as “nanofillers” is formed on the rubbing surface. Thus, the synergy of carbon cores and polyelectrolyte shells accounts for the outstanding tribological properties of PCDs.

In this article, we reported the synthesis of polyelectrolyte-modified carbon dots (PCDs) from chitosan. The physicochemical features of PCDs were determined by XRD, TEM, XPS, FTIR, and TGA measurements. The PCDs showed favorable dispersibility and fluorescence emission property in PEG200. Compared with PEG200, the formulated lubricant with an optimal dosage (0.25 wt%) of PCDs as lubricant additives demonstrated 39.1% and 63.6% reductions in average COFs and wear volumes, respectively. The service life of PEG200 can be prolonged by introducing PCDs, indicating the remarkable lubricity and durability of PCDs. Crucially, the friction regulation of PCDs under an external electric field (as low as ±1.0 V) was realized for the first time, which helps to disclose the indispensable function of polyelectrolyte shells in improving the tribological performances of PCDs. As evidenced by experiments and characterizations, the outstanding tribological behaviors of PCDs are attributed to the strong interfacial adsorption of polymeric shells, the nano-lubrication function of carbon cores, and the construction of the PCDs-embedded tribo-film of about 70 nm thickness. In summary, the synergy between polyelectrolytes and CDs plays an indispensable role in upgrading the tribological behaviors of PEG200. This study provides a new protocol to fabricate the PCDs-based additives using an inexpensive precursor and a facile synthetic route, which boosts the potential-controlled lubrication progress of nano-additives and the tribological application of CDs.

Zihao Mou: Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Data curation, Project administration, Writing – original draft. Jie Peng: Validation, Investigation. Ruixin Yan: Validation, Investigation. Qingbin Yang: Validation, Investigation. Bin Zhao: Characterization, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Dan Xiao: Supervision, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Song, J. Yan, H. Ji, Tribological performance of an imidazolium ionic liquid-functionalized SiO2@graphene oxide as an additive, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 13 (42) (2021) 50573-50583.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Compatibility between various ionic liquids and an organic friction modifier as lubricant additives
Langmuir

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Li, C. Kumara, H. M. Meyer III, H. Luo, J. Qu, Compatibility between various ionic liquids and an organic friction modifier as lubricant additives, Langmuir 34 (36) (2018) 10711-10720.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synergy between covalent organic frameworks and surfactants to promote water-based lubrication and corrosion resistance
ACS Appl. Nano Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Wen, Y. Lei, W. Li, M. Fan, Synergy between covalent organic frameworks and surfactants to promote water-based lubrication and corrosion resistance, ACS Appl. Nano Mater. 3 (2) (2020) 1400-1411.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multilayer tribofilm: an unique structure to strengthen interface tribological behaviors
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Wen, Y. Lei, Q. Yan, Y. Han, M. Fan, Multilayer tribofilm: an unique structure to strengthen interface tribological behaviors, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 13 (9) (2021) 11524-11534.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Importance of anionic reactive intermediates for lubricant component reactions with friction surfaces
Lubric. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Kajdas, Importance of anionic reactive intermediates for lubricant component reactions with friction surfaces, Lubr. Sci. 6 (3) (1994) 203-228.
